data.title: I Have Never Been More Bullish On LandBridge
data.description:
Introduction

Yesterday was not a good day for me. I'm not going to sugarcoat it.

As almost every single one of my readers knows, LandBridge (NYSE:LB) and Texas Pacific Land (TPL) are my largest and second-largest portoflio holdings, accounting for a combined 25% of my entire portfolio.

I have also given them a lot of attention in recent months, including in an article titled "Betting The Ranch: My 2 Largest Investments Just Got Even Bigger."

Well, as it turns out, they just got a lot smaller.

Looking at closing prices only, LandBridge shares made a new 2025 low, falling to prices not seen since October 2024. That's 42% below the 52-week high.

While I'm still up due to my first aggressive investment being the day after the IPO, that's no excuse for anything, as some of my bullish calls are under water, including the one titled "Why LandBridge Might Be The Smartest Investment I've Ever Made," published on May 12.

Now, I'm writing an update, as I believe the current wave of volatility requires a closer look at my LandBridge thesis, especially in light of high economic uncertainty, which has pressured oil prices and caused investors to believe the Permian Basin may be a case for the history books instead of a foundation for a bullish thesis.

So, as we have a lot to discuss, let's get to it!

A Very Unique Business Model

There are two companies on the market that own surface, water, and mineral rights in the Permian Basin. These companies are LandBridge and Texas Pacific. There are no other comparable companies. Not even in other basins.

So, right off the bat, I always have to bring up that we need to analyze the LandBridge Company as a very unique company. It's no usual mineral rights company that simply generates income from oil and gas companies drilling on its land. In fact, LandBridge does not produce anything, literally nothing.

How does it make money, you may ask?

Allow me to use the company's own words:

We generate revenue primarily from the use of our surface acreage, the sale of resources from our land and oil and natural gas royalties. The use of surface acreage generally includes easements or leases and various surface use royalties. Sale of resources generally includes sales of brackish water and other surface composite materials. Our assets consist mainly of fee surface acreage, oil and natural gas mineral interests, brackish water wells and ponds and related facilities. - LB 2Q25 10-Q

Essentially, LandBridge was founded to manage the land of WaterBridge, a private company fully owned by Five Point Energy, which is based in Houston, Texas, that owns a wide range of energy-related companies. This includes LandBridge, as Five Point has a 67% stake in LandBridge.

While LandBridge's land includes some mineral rights, oil and gas revenues account for less than 10% of its total revenues, making it an almost irrelevant segment of the business.

However, LandBridge is still very much dependent on the price of oil, as it benefits from almost every other operation on and under its land.

This includes royalty income from water pipelines on its land, resource sales for the drilling process, easement income from roads, solar, transmission lines, and everything related to the transportation and disposal of produced water.

The best thing is that LandBridge benefits from all of this without spending any major CapEx. For example, while WaterBridge may benefit more from water pricing, it pays royalty fees to LandBridge. The higher the demand for water services, the more money LandBridge makes.

This also protects it against inflation, as fees rise but costs remain low.

According to the company, this partnership provides it with access to valuable underutilized pore space in exchange for market-driven surface royalties and payments for infrastructure constructed on its land.

Pore space is needed to pump produced water back into the ground.

The water business is the number one reason why I own this company, as water is the biggest bottleneck in the American energy industry. This is what I wrote in my just-released article on Texas Pacific Land:

In some areas of the Permian (mainly in the Delaware Basin), every barrel of oil comes with up to ten barrels of water. As oil producers are drilling deeper, water is only becoming a bigger problem, leading to the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas reporting that the majority of oil companies see long-term constraints from this water problem.

Not to sound dramatic, but given that the Permian was the core of the shale revolution that provided the world with nearly unprecedented oil supply growth between the Great Financial Crisis and the pandemic, Permian water is a global bottleneck problem that needs to be taken care of.

As I showed in that article, drillers are willing to pay a lot of money for water disposal, as prices have risen from roughly $0.15 in 2010 to $1.20 in 2025.

Even though I have spent a lot of time on this subject, I believe it is still one of the most underestimated factors in energy, as the biggest supply growth engine is now pressured by toxic water.

On top of dealing with water, LandBridge has always had the goal of creating Powered Land solutions, which is the description it gave its idea of attractive commercial players to the Permian Basin. After all, the Permian is very remote, it has an abundance of cheap energy, nearly limitless cooling water, access to high-quality fiber, and a very friendly regulatory environment.

This is perfect for data centers.

Although LandBridge already works with one data center partner, the goal is to get the really big guys on board, which are the >2-5 GW data center campuses expected to power the next wave of AI.

As it turns out, there's progress in both water and data centers.

I Disagree With The Market, 2Q25 Was Great

Allow me to start this part by throwing some numbers at you.

In the second quarter, revenues came in at $47.5 million, 8% more compared to 1Q25 and 83% more compared to the prior-year quarter. This performance was supported by a 31% sequential increase in surface use royalties and revenues thanks to renewal payments, new project easements, and more commercial activity on its land.

As we can see below, this is the only segment with substantial growth, as resource sales and royalties were up just 16% year-over-year and down on a sequential basis. That's the impact of subdued oil and gas drilling activity.

Below $70 WTI, most oil and gas drillers are very defensive, as it costs roughly $61-$62 WTI to profitably drill a new well in the Permian. At current prices, "nobody" is willing to sacrifice valuable reserves.

These subdued prices also explain why royalty income was down both on a sequential and year-over-year basis. But then again, these account for just 7% of total revenues. We can almost ignore them.

Nonetheless, LandBridge remained strong. On top of growth in revenues and EBITDA, the company was able to report 89% adjusted EBITDA margins, a 76% free cash flow margin, and lower its net debt to 2.4x, within its target range of 2.0x to 2.5x EBITDA.

On a side note, the company's 10-Q was released on the day before its official earnings release (as announced on the website). While I found this incredibly unprofessional, it indicates that the stock did not sell off because of these numbers. After all, they were available a full trading day before the release.

I also doubt it was due to new developments, as the company had three major announcements:

  • A major deal is a 10-year surface use and pore space reservation agreement with Devon Energy (DVN), which secures 300,000 barrels per day of pore space capacity on East Stateline and Speed Ranches for water takeaway and disposal. That's starting in 2Q27. While it takes two years for revenues to come in, this agreement includes a minimum commitment of 175,000 barrels per day and reflects the growing importance of pore space for operators in the New Mexico Delaware Basin, which is why Devon is closing deals this early. Producers are simply "panicking" when it comes to finding future pore spaces for produced water.
  • LandBridge signed an option agreement with a large public independent power producer ("IPP") for a natural gas-fired combined cycle gas turbine ("CCGT") plant in Reeves County to support future data center power demands. Please note that this does not indicate the company is getting a major new data center deal soon. But that's OK for reasons I'll explain in a second. What matters here is that it's a clear sign that data centers are coming to the Permian. IPPs need to be prepared for that. According to LandBridge, we can expect this currently anonymous IPP to make an official statement and explain its plans in greater detail.
  • LandBridge announced a strategic partnership with a leading vertically integrated power generation provider that aims to develop scalable, resilient, and sustainable energy infrastructure. As you can imagine, this too is bullish for commercial power demand in the area for, e.g., data centers.

I believe one of the reasons why LandBridge sold off is that it did not announce a major data center deal. However, as bullish as I am on data centers moving to the Permian, I am not at all surprised by that.

For starters, the infrastructure isn't there yet. Dealing with IPPs to develop infrastructure is always the first step. Note that this is never done without a clear indication that demand is high, as no sane energy manager will spend hundreds of millions on infrastructure without the possibility of making money.

A few months ago, Five Point Energy founded PowerBridge, a private company that deals with data center clients and the providers of energy. Essentially, it was founded to streamline data center onboarding on LandBridge land.

Mr. Hernandez said, “We are excited to partner with Five Point to capitalize on this generational moment in the growth and convergence of energy and digital infrastructure. With Five Point’s institutional support, our collective energy infrastructure, power operations, data center development, and fiber experience, and the significant additional value that other Five Point portfolio companies can bring for customers, we look forward to providing hyperscale customers with turnkey data center campus solutions at scale.” - Businesswire

During the LandBridge earnings call, an analyst asked why we hear from data centers being built all over the place except for the Permian. That's a very valid question. So far, the only major deal we have heard is the one from OpenAI, which is building a mega data center in West Texas.

The exchange even got a bit tense when the analyst (Lawrence Goldstein) questioned the validity of LandBridge's comments that it takes time to get Big Tech used to the idea of building something in the Permian.

And yet you say they -- it takes a while to learn about what you have in the way of assets, everything available and everything at the lowest prices. And in Oregon or the Pacific Northwest, Washington, there, they never heard in those places either. But it's so obvious what your assets are, and yet we've not heard a single company. So when you say it takes a while for them to learn about it, -- with all due respect, come on. They haven't heard of Texas. They haven't heard... - Lawrence Goldstein (LB 2Q25 Earnings Call)

While I also wouldn't mind a big data center announcement, these deals take time. For example, we won't see an announcement until everything has been signed. If LandBridge were to announce a deal too early, it could lead to third parties buying land close to the data center, creating new stakeholders that need to be dealt with. The risk of that is just too big.

Also, data center deals close to densely populated areas always get a lot of attention, as journalists can get good views. People usually don't want to live next to data centers. The news of future developments tends to create big headlines for regional stakeholders.

As "nobody" lives in the Permian, there isn't a big need for coverage. Most of these deals are flying below the radar until the point where the Big Tech companies that fund these projects feel the need to make it public.

Moreover, this stuff takes time. Working with companies like PowerBridge, power producers, infrastructure companies, engineers, and others can take many months, if not years.

After all, constructing a multi-gigawatt data center is different from building smaller units. Finding suitable land alone could take more than a year.

Last but not least, we need to be aware that some producers of natural gas turbines have multi-year waitlists. It can take years until a data center can be built in most areas. That's why the data center thesis is a long-term thesis. I never ever promised we would get deals rolling in like drive-through orders at your local fast food franchise.

Hardware simply isn't readily available yet.

I just wish LandBridge had communicated this better during their earnings call. I really felt they were a bit unprepared for these questions. After all, educating the public on this super important issue is important as well.

With that said, I remain super bullish on the data center business, and I'm very glad to see the company is dealing with IPPs. In the weeks ahead, I think we will get a lot more data on this.

Then there's the ruling on pore space.

Earlier this year, the Texas Supreme Court ruled that pore space under the surface belonged to surface owners instead of mineral rights owners. This could be a big deal, as pore space is super valuable, given the increasing takeaway and storage issues of produced water.

For LandBridge, this isn't an issue, as its mostly contiguous acreage allows for distributed injection, which reduces pressure and improves operational efficiency. According to the company, this regulatory environment reinforces its differentiated value proposition and supports its long-term success in the Permian Basin.

I agree with them, as they have some of the best land for water injection, allowing them to utilize a lot of land without running into capacity headwinds.

Even better, when it comes to pore space, the company's capacity exceeds 5 million barrels per day, with ongoing efforts to acquire additional underutilized land. Currently, the company's geological teams are focused on expanding its footprint to meet growing demand, especially in the Delaware Basin, which has become the hotspot for oil production growth in the Permian.

As we can see below, the company's land is perfectly located next to the prime spots for drilling in the area, attracting high demand for water handling and injection operations, as well as easements for pipelines and support assets.

Another thing the market seems to dislike is that projects for LandBridge take time to materialize. For example, the Devon Energy deal starts to make money in 2027. Data centers are also a multi-year deal.

But that's OK.

LandBridge isn't a fast food chain where all it takes is putting a few more patties on the grill to increase output.

According to the company, power and renewable projects, like the DBR Solar and IPP deals, have longer lead times (12–24 months or more), while water and energy infrastructure projects, like the BPX Kraken and Speedway projects, can contribute EBITDA more quickly.

Meanwhile, the Speedway project, which is expected to be fully FID-ed soon, could generate up to $30 million in royalty cash flow once it's fully operational, with contributions starting as early as late 2025 or early 2026.

Speedway is a WaterBridge program, and one of many projects I expect to facilitate the Delaware Basin's booming water demand.

While it seems the market wants even bigger announcements every quarter, investors are missing the point that all of this is based on gradual growth.

And that's OK, as LandBridge is already profitable and seeing steady growth.

The problem is that because it depends on the timing of larger deals, guidance was adjusted. It now sees an adjusted EBITDA range of $160 million to $180 million. That's down $10 million from the prior $170-$190 million range.

In anticipation of the execution of the DBR Solar opportunity with a large public renewable energy developer and operator, we are adjusting our guidance for fiscal year 2025 to an Adjusted EBITDA range between $160 million and $180 million. This adjustment is primarily driven by an expectation that the majority of revenue associated with the DBR Solar opportunity will be recognized following this year. - LB 2Q25 Earnings Release

So, what does this mean for shareholders?

Valuation

LandBridge has a market cap of $3.9 billion. It has roughly $350 million in net debt, giving it an enterprise value of roughly $4,350 million.

This is 26x the EBITDA guidance midpoint.

The only comparable company, Texas Pacific Land, has traded at roughly 20-25x EBITDA for more than 25 years.

While some may make the case that 25x EBITDA is high, bear in mind that we are dealing with a company that turns close to 80 cents of every revenue dollar into free cash flow with close to zero in CapEx requirements.

It's also a company where growth is expected to pick up considerably after 2025 due to the recently announced deals and what I expect will be a lot more demand for water handling and power-related operations.

Based on the fully diluted share count, the company is expected to generate close to $2.20-$2.30 in EBITDA per share this year. For now, FactSet expectations are for a surge to $3.45 in 2027, which would imply a multiple of a mere 15x EBITDA.

Even based on ultra-conservative estimates, this would give the stock a fair price target of $70, 40% above the current price.

  • This does not include any data center deals.
  • It does not include any new water deals.
  • It does not incorporate bigger deals with IPPs.

I expect all of these things to materialize in the years ahead.

Also note that the Speedway project alone could generate $30 million in annual revenue, which is conservative and could translate to nearly $27 million in EBITDA (90% margin).

That's why I wrote on X (formerly known as Twitter) in a reply to someone else that LandBridge investors are basically getting a great deal for a fast-growing water company, with the entire data center thesis for free.

While I am a bit annoyed by the poorly executed earnings call and the fact that the company had to lower guidance, which was more of a bad look than bad financial news, I am convinced LandBridge remains one of the most misunderstood investment opportunities.

And yes, while yesterday hurt me a lot, I am as convinced as ever that we're dealing with a company with an incredibly bright future.

The most important thing to keep in mind, though, is that this is a volatile beast and that it requires time to unlock value. LandBridge's operations take years to unfold. But once they do, I think we've only seen the start of the bull case since last year.

Takeaway

LandBridge isn't just another energy play. It's a unique business, built as a toll road for Permian water, infrastructure, and, eventually, data centers.

All of it without any large CapEx requirements, as it simply allows others to use its land.

Even better, while low oil prices cloud the picture for Permian growth, the company continues to win big deals.

  • The Devon deal? A long-term win.
  • The IPP partnerships? Proof that demand is coming.
  • Even the Texas pore space ruling reinforces its advantage.

While the stock is volatile and big deals take time, and I'm not thrilled about the guidance tweak or the messy earnings call, at 26x EBITDA and ultra-high margins, I think this stock is the cheapest investment I own.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risk is a "lower for longer" oil price environment, which could get more oil companies to play defense, pressuring water handling and resource sales demand.

Other risks include the company's inability to get new deals, although these risks are limited, as LandBridge owns "mission-critical" land. It would have to mess up really big to erase interest from third parties in its services.

Also, as I have written in my TPL article, Permian reserves remain healthy:

Moreover, to support my point, the company cited a report by Inveris that estimates more than 60,000 drilling locations with breakevens below $60 WTI and $3 natural gas, which represent more than 30 billion barrels of oil.

At the 2024 drilling pace of 5,700 wells per year, this would translate to an 11-year inventory for low-breakeven wells, with additional locations becoming economic at higher oil prices.

Last but not least, I believe the 20% short interest in the stock will be unwound in the quarters ahead, as the short ratio surged when the stock traded close to $80-$90.

To me, these are mainly speculative positions of investors "shorting the Permian."

Also, note that LB is super volatile. Do not buy it if you cannot handle it or if you do not have the time/patience to own it for multiple years.

data.title: RBA Preview: Data Supports A Rate Cut Next Week
data.description:
By Deepali Bhargava & Francesco Pesole

Weaker data, following RBA’s cautious stance, should give way to a rate cut

We expect the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) to cut the cash rate by 25 basis points to 3.6% at its upcoming policy meeting on 12 August. Recent data on growth and inflation have come in softer than expected, reinforcing the case for easing.

In a surprise move last month, the RBA held the cash rate steady at 3.85%, defying both market consensus and our own expectations. The decision reflected the central bank’s desire for more evidence that inflation is tracking sustainably toward its 2.5% target. Since then, both the second quarter inflation report and June employment data have undershot expectations, suggesting that the RBA’s cautious stance in July may now give way to a rate cut.

Data points on both growth and inflation support a rate cut

Headline CPI inflation continued to ease in June, coming in at 1.9% year-on-year, while trimmed mean CPI fell to 2.1%. On a quarterly basis, headline inflation moderated to 2.1% in the second quarter, down from 2.4% in the first quarter. The slowdown was largely driven by softer services inflation, particularly in financial and insurance services, as well as lower transport prices.

Labour market data also disappointed. Employment rose by just 2,000 in June, well below the consensus expectation of 20,000, while the unemployment rate edged up to 4.3% from 4.1% in May. Full-time employment contracted by 38,000, offset by a 40,000 increase in part-time jobs, which tend to be less stable and lower paying. Hours worked fell 0.9% month-on-month in June, also suggesting a weaker labour market.

Employment growth has been trending downwards

The market angle: pricing still leaning dovish

Markets were fully pricing in a rate cut at the August meeting within hours of July’s surprise hold. As discussed, recent data has largely validated short-term rate-cut bets but has failed to shift expectations for future easing. The AUD OIS curve (chart below) remains largely unchanged since the day after the July meeting, with approximately 62 basis points priced in for year-end and a terminal rate around 3.00–3.10%.

We remain sceptical that the RBA will provide sufficiently strong guidance to push markets toward fully pricing in or out an additional 25-basis point cut in December (currently at 40%) or to bring forward the next move from November to September. The RBA appears as uncertain as markets regarding the disinflation path, while trade uncertainty remains elevated ahead of US pharmaceutical tariff announcements, which could impact Australian exports.

Our call remains for only one additional cut by year-end following the expected August reduction, making us slightly more hawkish than market pricing. This supports our bullish view on AUD/USD, although the USD leg remains dominant. We have revised our AUD/USD forecast higher, now targeting 0.67 by year-end.

RBA cash rate pricing unchanged in the past month

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Wasatch Emerging Markets Select Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Overview

Following a sharp initial selloff tied to global trade tensions, emerging-market equities recovered to deliver solid second-quarter returns. The benchmark MSCI Emerging Markets Index finished 11.99% higher, driven by outsized gains in the tech-heavy stock markets of Korea and Taiwan. The Wasatch Emerging Markets Select Fund—Investor Class outperformed the benchmark, logging a quarterly total return of 12.45%.

Although the quarter opened on a weak note, sentiment improved after the Trump administration put new reciprocal tariffs on hold for 90 days. That shift enabled the Index to recapture lost ground by early May, with gains continuing through the remainder of the quarter. Korea led all Index constituents during the period, making the Fund’s underweight there the largest source of weakness relative to the benchmark. Fund holdings in Taiwan returned nearly 25% but modestly lagged the benchmark in that country, and slight underexposure to Taiwan during the quarter also hindered relative results.

India was a source of relative strength, powered by effective stock selection in a market that returned to positive territory after two consecutive quarters of declines. Renewed investor optimism followed stronger-than-expected GDP growth, which accelerated to 7.4% in the March quarter. Easing inflation pressures enabled the Reserve Bank of India, the country’s central bank, to lower interest rates, which fueled investment activity. Additionally, India’s limited reliance on exports helped insulate its economy from global trade tensions.

Brazil was the Fund’s biggest source of strength against the benchmark, bolstered by healthy gains in two of our larger positions. Both holdings outpaced the Brazilian positions in the Index and made outsized contributions to the Fund’s return. China had minimal impact on relative performance. While benchmark positions in China posted a modest gain, weaker stock selection in the Fund was offset by a significant underweight.

Details of the quarter

The strongest contributor to Fund performance for the quarter was ASPEED Technology, Inc. The Taiwan-based fabless chip designer reported 104% sales growth and a 126% year-over-year surge in earnings per share for its most-recent quarter, supported by margin expansion and tighter cost controls. The company’s management also signaled a potential recovery in market share previously lost because of differing product upgrade cycles relative to competitors.

MercadoLibre, Inc. (MELI) was also a top contributor. The company operates e-commerce and fintech platforms in Brazil and other Latin American countries, with particularly strong momentum in Argentina during the most recent quarter. In that market, stabilizing macroeconomic conditions supported a sharp increase in revenue and double-digit year-over-year profit growth as the company capitalized on its dominant position. Continued profitability in Argentina is expected to allow expansion efforts in Brazil and Mexico to proceed as planned.

Another strong stock in the Fund was NU Holdings Ltd. (NU). A Brazilian firm, NU operates Latin America’s largest fintech bank. Despite rising credit risks across Brazil’s financial sector, NU maintained solid asset quality and continued to grow its domestic business. Management also noted plans to accelerate growth initiatives in Mexico during the second half of the year.

The greatest detractor from Fund performance for the quarter was Meituan (OTCPK:MPNGF). The company operates leading online platforms for meal delivery, consumer products and retail services in China. Shares of Meituan declined moderately as investor concerns surfaced around rising competitive pressure and around the potential impact on margins from increased investment and discounting. Additional short-term expenses tied to Meituan’s early-stage expansion in the Middle East further weighed on sentiment. Despite these challenges, we remain constructive on the stock, especially considering the company’s strong position in the markets it serves.

Globant SA (GLOB) also detracted as the global information-technology (IT) consulting firm continued to face a challenging operating environment. Globant’s management had lowered guidance earlier in the year and then cut its full-year forecast again in May following quarterly revenue and earnings that fell short of expectations. Despite these near-term headwinds, we believe Globant remains well-positioned to gain from increasing adoption of artificial intelligence (AI) and automation. With many corporate clients still in the early stages of integrating these technologies, we view the company as a likely long-term beneficiary.

Another weak stock in the Fund was Trip.com Group Ltd. (TCOM). Based in China, the company operates one of the world’s leading one-stop travel platforms. Margins came in slightly below expectations in Trip.com’s most recent quarter as it continued investing in foreign markets that are not yet profitable. However, recent conversations with company management were encouraging. Trip.com’s leadership team remains measured in its approach and expressed confidence that profitability is poised to improve. (Current and future holdings are subject to risk and change.)

Outlook and positioning

As we look ahead to the second half of the year, the market backdrop remains somewhat less predictable, with renewed geopolitical uncertainty adding to the mix. Uncertainties around trade policy pose another major wildcard.

Reflecting our continued emphasis on in-depth, on-the-ground research, we’ve stepped up our travel to Taiwan and India—the Fund’s two most heavily weighted geographies. We recently completed our second trip to Taiwan this year, with our efforts focused on rapid developments in AI. We avoided chasing stocks during the island’s AI- driven rally, believing that the real-world application of these technologies remains in its early stages—and that much of the runup in so-called “AI stocks” was speculative rather than rooted in fundamentals.

With the help of two new senior hires, we’re mapping the entire value chain surrounding AI and advanced semiconductors in Taiwan—from test pins to cooling systems and cabling for AI servers. We’re seeking companies whose growth is powered by AI, advanced computing and the surge in capital spending that has accompanied this wave of innovation. Our objective is to identify reasonably valued businesses positioned to benefit from durable trends and capable of delivering strong revenue and earnings growth over time. A few of these names have already been added to the Fund.

During our recent two-week trip to India—our second of three planned for this year—we visited a wide range of companies across Bangalore, Coimbatore, Chennai, Delhi and Jaipur. The itinerary included both existing holdings and prospective investments. Meetings with current portfolio companies helped clarify key questions and generally reinforced our investment theses. We also met with firms under consideration for purchase across sectors such as IT, industrials, financials and health care. These conversations uncovered a broad set of high-quality, reasonably valued businesses that we believe are well suited for future inclusion in the Fund. A trip is planned for July to several smaller emerging markets in the Middle East and Europe. Planned stops include Lithuania, Poland, Morocco and Greece.

Evaluating management teams remains a cornerstone of our investment process. We believe our face-to-face meetings help us distinguish good management teams from truly exceptional ones—a distinction we view as critical to delivering long-term results.

Thank you for the opportunity to manage your assets.

Sincerely,

Ajay Krishnan, Stuart Rigby, Scott Thomas and Anh Hoang

An investor should consider investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus, containing this and other information, visit wasatchglobal.com or call 800.551.1700. Please read the prospectus carefully before investing.

Information in this document regarding market or economic trends, or the factors influencing historical or future performance, reflects the opinions of management as of the date of this document. These statements should not be relied upon for any other purpose.

The Wasatch Emerging Markets Select Fund’s investment objective is long-term growth of capital. CFA® is a trademark owned by the CFA Institute.

data.title: Premium Brands Holding Corp. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Premium Brands Holding Corp. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Berkshire's Q2 Earnings: Better Than We Were Led To Believe
data.description:
Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.B)(BRK.A) released its second quarter earnings on Saturday, August 2. Although the release beat expectations for the headline metrics (i.e. operating earnings per share and revenue), it nevertheless triggered a selloff in Berkshire Hathaway shares, which underperformed the S&P 500 by 2.5% in the four days ended Thursday August 7. The price momentum after Berkshire's earnings came out seemed to indicate dimming investor sentiment toward Berkshire Hathaway shares.

Despite that, the earnings release itself was not all bad. It did show negative growth in both revenue and operating income, but the insurance segment's profitability remained sky-high, with an 84.5 combined ratio across all the insurance businesses, and an 81.7% combined ratio at GEICO. Also, despite the negative growth in the quarter, more severely negative growth was expected, as Berkshire's second quarter operating income per share ($5.17) was listed as a beat.

So, Berkshire's second quarter release was ahead of estimates, yet the market's immediate reaction was to sell off the stock, which remains down from last Friday's closing price. This might mean that there is an opportunity to buy Berkshire at a discounted price. Assuming the market was right about Berkshire's fair value before the second quarter release came out, it would appear to have been wrong about it afterward, as the stock sold off despite the release being better than the already-priced in expectations.

With all that being said, investors have valid reasons for scrutinizing Berkshire more closely than they did in the past. The company's famed CEO Warren Buffett announced his retirement at the last shareholder meeting, and will be formally stepping down at the end of this year. This is significant because Buffett's capital allocation skills have long been seen as Berkshire's key differentiator. The company's soon-to-be CEO Greg Abel comes with high praise from Warren Buffett, who whole-heartedly endorsed Abel to lead Berkshire after he is gone. However, Abel is not known as a stock picker, and some worry that he might not do as good a job as Buffett at managing Berkshire's investment portfolio.

Only time will tell how Abel actually performs as CEO of Berkshire Hathaway. In the meantime, we have hard evidence that the company is still earning high underwriting profits; is still managing risks prudently; and is still long many stocks that Buffett picked himself. On a less positive note, the company took a $3.8 billion writedown on its Kraft Heinz (KHC) investment in the second quarter bringing total writedowns on that investment to $6.8 billion since it was initiated. With its stock price down roughly 66% from its 2017 average, Kraft Heinz may never recover to the level that Buffett paid for it. That just goes to show that even Warren Buffett makes mistakes sometimes.

When I last covered Berkshire Hathaway, I rated the stock a strong buy on the grounds that Greg Abel was likely to become a good steward of Berkshire upon assumption of the CEO office. Since then, Berkshire stock has gone down in price, and I have continued holding my shares. On the whole, I remain bullish on Berkshire stock. However, a couple of red flags in Berkshire's second quarter earnings release dim my enthusiasm for the company somewhat. Accordingly, I am downgrading my Berkshire rating to buy. In the ensuing paragraphs I explain my rationale for this choice.

Berkshire Hathaway Q2 Earnings Recap

Berkshire Hathaway's second quarter earnings release beat analyst expectations on all of the headline metrics, and showed good results in most segments. However, there were some sour points in the release as well. Some highlight metrics included:

  • $92.515 billion in revenue, down 1.2% (beat by $10.29 billion).

  • $11.16 billion in operating income, down 3.8%.

  • $5.73 in reported earnings per share (EPS), down 59.3%.

  • $22.19 billion in insurance premiums, up 0.11%.

  • $2 billion in railroad operating earnings, up 10.6%.

  • A $3.8 billion impairment on the Kraft Heinz investment, bringing total impairments on that investment to $6.8 billion since inception.

Overall, it was a satisfactory release. While the reported EPS figure might look alarming, it's important to remember that that figure incorporates the effects of unpredictable non-recurring non-cash items like stock portfolio fluctuations and hedging derivative losses. It isn't always that meaningful, so Berkshire's operating earnings are likely more instructive than its reported earnings.

Berkshire's operating earnings fell in the second quarter too, but the magnitude of decline in that case was much lesser and reflective of increased costs in some areas (e.g. property insurance claims) rather than poor revenue performance. With that established, we can move on to Berkshire Hathaway's long-term revenue and profit drivers.

Long Term Drivers

Over long periods of time, Berkshire Hathaway's earnings tend to be influenced by four main factors (excluding the macroeconomic factors that all companies are subject to):

  1. Insurance premiums.

  2. Natural disasters (Berkshire is a property and casualty insurer).

  3. Energy prices (the company has been heavily betting on oil stocks like Occidental Petroleum (OXY) lately).

  4. Stock market performance (insofar as we count the company's GAAP earnings as economically meaningful).

How are these factors likely to influence Berkshire's results going forward?

Insurance premiums are partially at the discretion of individual insurers, but insurers have to remain competitive with others in the same industry, which places a natural limit on rates. On a forward looking basis, 2025's high catastrophe losses are expected to lead to higher premiums in 2026.

Natural disasters also tend to influence Berkshire's earnings, and were unusually high in the 2025 period. These resulted in non-GEICO insurance EBIT declining considerably in the second quarter. Premiums have not caught up with claims yet, but as previously mentioned, higher premiums are expected to come next year. So, we might see Berkshire's underwriting income improve relative to losses in 2026.

Energy prices are a major factor in Berkshire's future earnings because the company has been betting heavily on oil stocks like Occidental Petroleum lately. The fundamentals for oil can be summarized as follows: oil demand is growing (albeit slowly) in 2025, while OPEC is hiking supply, also at a slow face. These two factors viewed side by side tend to argue for oil prices staying within the $60-$70 (WTI) range they've bounced around in recent months.

Last but not least, we have stock market performance, which is a factor in Berkshire's GAAP earnings but not its operating earnings. I've touched on this extensively in past articles: basically I think the S&P 500 is overheated today and due for a correction. If I'm right, then stock returns will probably weigh on Berkshire's performance in the months ahead; however, the company has managed risks well, cashing out many top performing stocks recently to move into treasuries.

The facts above collectively argue for Berkshire's operating earnings trending up in the next 12 months, though possibly with some weakness in the equity portfolio.

Berkshire Stock Valuation

Having looked at Berkshire's recent earnings and future prospects, we can now proceed to looking at its multiples.

In the last four quarters, Berkshire earned (in operating income per B share):

  • Q2: $5.17.

  • Q1: $4.46.

  • Q4: $6.37.

  • Q3: $4.68.

These amounts sum to $20.68. At Thursday's closing price, they give Berkshire a price/operating earnings ratio of 22. That's lower than the market right now, but higher than average for financials. Overall, it argues that Berkshire is cheap but not deep value. Some other multiples can be seen below, courtesy of Seeking Alpha Quant:

Again, these multiples are broadly lower than those of the market, but not by a wide margin. By the standards of financial stocks, they collectively are about average, with the P/E ratio being a little higher than average.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on Berkshire's second quarter results is that they showed continued resilience in the company's core operations and improvement in several segments. While earnings fell on a year over year basis, they grew sequentially, and a few businesses (i.e. GEICO and BNSF) delivered truly superior results. At 22 times earnings, Berkshire is a far cry from deep value. Nevertheless, it is a decent value for the price of admission investors are paying today.

data.title: Vermilion Energy: A Solid Q2 And The Valuation Remains Very Attractive
data.description:
Overview

Vermillion Energy (NYSE:VET)(TSX:VET:CA) is a Canadian mid-cap oil & natural gas producer. The company's exposure has changed some over the last year following the Westbrick Energy acquisition in late 2024 and some divestments of its liquids-weighted assets in the United States and Saskatchewan.

The company still has a material exposure to Europe and Australia, where the margins continue to be very attractive, but Vermillion has become much more natural gas weighted recently.

The Westbrick Energy acquisition has added quite a bit of Canadian natural gas production, where the margins might improve following the commissioning of LNG Canada lately. So far though, Canadian natural gas continues to trade at a material discount to U.S. natural gas, even if the futures curve for Canadian natural gas is relatively constructive a few months out.

I have covered Vermilion frequently over the last few years and my prior articles can be found here. Today's article will focus on the Q2 2025 results that were released yesterday after the close and there will be a conference call at 11 am ET today, which can be found here.

The stock price performance of Vermilion has been poor lately, both this year and over the last few years. The underperformance a couple of years ago was primarily due to lower energy prices and especially lower European natural gas prices, but the underperformance is also because Vermilion now trades with a lower valuation multiple than many peers.

The underperformance this year is likely explained by the large debt-financed acquisition that has led to a higher financial leverage, which is not viewed positively by the market when energy prices have weakened. With that said, the valuation continues to be very appealing for Vermillion.

Q2 2025 Results

Vermilion reported production of 136,002 boe/d during Q2, up 32% compared to the prior quarter and at the upper end of the quarterly guidance range. A full quarter with the Westbrick Energy assets is the primary reason for the increase in production.

Just about all the production growth was in Canada and consequently North America, where the production increased by 44% while the international production grew a marginal 1% in Q2 compared to Q1.

Among the international countries, we saw a 19% increase in Germany and an increase in Croatia quarter-over-quarter, which was offset by lower production in a few of the other European countries.

With more Canadian natural gas production added to the portfolio, we have seen a significant decline in the funds flow in Canadian dollar per barrel of oil equivalent in North America. It was C$12/boe in Q2 compared to C$17/boe in the prior quarter. However, note that this is for continuing operations in the most recent quarter, so the U.S. and Saskatchewan assets are excluded from this figure.

The funds flow for the international segment was C$49/boe in Q2, down 27% compared to Q1, mostly due to lower energy prices during much of Q2. Even if we have seen notable declines quarter-over-quarter in funds flow across the board, all international countries, apart from France, still had a funds flow above C$40/boe in Q2, which is of course impressive.

The funds flow and free cash flow were C$260M and C$165M in the most recent quarter, where funds flow increased 1% compared to the prior quarter and free cash flow increased substantially more, even if that figure was impacted by lower capex in the quarter.

The funds flow per share was C$1.69 and the free cash flow per share was C$0.94. The excess free cash flow, which is free cash flow minus payments on lease obligations and asset retirement obligations settled, was C$0.86.

Vermilion pays a quarterly dividend of C$0.13 that equates to a dividend yield of 4.8%. The company has also been buying back shares at a decent pace over the last couple of years. However, with the recent spike in net debt, we can likely expect the buybacks to be relatively muted in the near term. At least unless we see substantially higher energy prices.

Q2 2025 was a relatively good quarter, with little to dislike. The net debt has following the asset divestments come down to C$1.4B and it is expected to finish the year around C$1.3B. Vermilion reported a net loss of C$233M in Q2, which included a net loss of C$308M from discontinued operations, reflecting a non-cash adjustment to the book value.

Conclusion

Using the latest share price and financials as of Q2 2025, Vermillion has a market cap of C$1.7B and an enterprise value of C$3.1B.

Vermillion has generated around C$500-600M in free cash flow over the last couple of years. This year will be lower due to weaker energy prices, likely to be somewhere around C$350M. That translates to a free cash flow yield of 21% against the market cap and 13% against the enterprise value.

I do think somewhere around C$500M is a more reasonable free cash flow estimate in a normalized market, which would take the free cash flow yield to a massive 30% against the market cap and 18% against the enterprise value.

So, Vermilion is cheap based on current cash flows, but also very cheap in what I think is more of a normalized market. I consequently rate Vermilion as a strong buy at this level, even if the stock price has had a decent bounce from the lows in April.

There is commodity price risk with Vermilion, but its exposure to a few different markets and good hedges over the next couple of years makes the company relatively resilient during a period of weaker commodity prices.

The company has an elevated financial leverage following the Westbrick Energy acquisition, even if the recent asset divestments have decreased the financial leverage some. This is a risk to be aware of though and something I am relatively comfortable with. It will likely keep the buybacks more muted in the near term unless we see substantially higher energy prices.

data.title: STOP Buying These 3 Dividend Stocks (And Buy These Instead)
data.description:
Co-written by Austin Rogers for High Yield Investor

Popularity does not equal quality.

There are plenty of popular dividend stocks that are not worth owning as long-term investments. Just as there are different reasons for buying a stock, there are many different reasons to avoid a stock as well.

Reasons to avoid a dividend stock include:

  • High payout ratios that may indicate unsustainable dividends (aka "yield traps")
  • A high debt load
  • A large wall of near-term debt maturities
  • A high cost of capital, including both debt (credit rating) and equity (stock valuation)
  • Highly cyclical revenues and cash flows
  • Lack of organic growth prospects
  • Lack of reasonably valued external/inorganic growth opportunities
  • Failure to rejuvenate growth even after multiple management changes
  • A major acquisition that increases debt or ventures into another business line outside the company's core competency
  • One's portfolio is already too heavily exposed to a certain sector or industry, such as energy, utilities, or real estate

A common mistake dividend investors make is owning a stock merely for the dividend.

"Who cares if there's high debt and no growth?" the investor thinks. "It pays a high dividend yield, and that's all I'm looking for."

The danger in this thinking is that high debt, lack of growth, and/or cyclicality can create a negative self-reinforcing loop in which lack of positive momentum aggravates the problems of debt, low growth, or cyclicality.

It keeps the valuation low, the cost of capital high, and reduces the company's ability to pull itself out of its self-reinforcing cycle of a weak balance sheet and poor growth prospects.

While there are some exceptional cases of companies that can remain fundamentally rangebound for a long period of time, it is generally the case that if a company isn't growing, it is dying.

It may be a rapid death or a slow death, but lack of growth tends to be a death knell all the same.

With that said, here are three dividend stocks we would avoid, followed by three others we would buy today.

3 Stocks To Avoid

1. Realty Income (O)

When it comes to popular dividend stocks, it doesn't get much more popular than O, the self-designated "Monthly Dividend Company."

On the surface, there's a lot to like about O, and there are good reasons why dividend investors have flocked to it over the last few decades.

It's a Dividend Aristocrat with a 30-year dividend growth streak. It enjoys an A- credit rating, which few other REITs can boast. And it has a proven track record of being a recession-resistant stock, given the nature of its long-term net leases that require tenants to pay for all or most property maintenance, insurance, and taxes.

Moreover, in the two decades leading up to 2022, O generated astoundingly strong total returns, beating both the S&P 500 (SPY) and the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ):

What's not to like about this net lease stalwart?

For starters, O is sensitive to movements in interest rates and thrives most in a low interest rate environment. The REIT has a steady slate of debt maturities every year for the foreseeable future, and these loans will most likely be refinanced at higher interest rates, creating a refinancing headwind as far out as the eye can see.

Plus, higher interest rates and the resultingly lower equity valuation that comes with them together reduce the REIT's all-important investment spread between its cost of capital and its acquisition yields. This further reduces growth.

Notice that since 2022, O and the broader real estate sector have dramatically underperformed the S&P 500:

Rising interest rates were by far the biggest reason for this.

Another point to note about O is its sheer size. The REIT's portfolio is about $70 billion in total, making it bigger than the next 10 largest net lease REITs combined.

We think O has become a victim of its own success in multiple ways:

  1. Given the nature of its leases, O has minimal organic growth of no more than 1-1.5%. And the sheer magnitude of its portfolio makes it difficult to acquire enough real estate to generate external (acquisitive) growth.
  2. Given the need to widen the acquisition dragnet, management likely need to lower the underwriting standards of acquisitions or venture outside their sphere of competence for investments. We think both are happening.
  3. Lack of portfolio pruning in the past (in order to focus on growth) has resulted in overexposure to several risky tenants in the pharmacy, movie theater, and dollar store spaces.

With these unavoidable headwinds in view, we think investors would do well to avoid "The Monthly Dividend Company." While the REIT surely has a fantastic historical track record, we think its future will look much different than its past.

2. AT&T (T)

Since offloading its ill-conceived investment in Warner Media a few years ago, this telecommunications giant has taken significant steps to streamline its operations, reduce debt, and refocus on its core competency.

We applaud the company for these smart moves, and so has the market, having doubled the company's valuation over the last few years:

But virtually the entirety of T's rally over the last few years can be attributed to multiple expansion.

We admittedly don't know what the fair valuation should be for T, but we think it is well below its current ~13x forward P/E ratio. Historically, the company has traded at an 8-10x multiple. Even if you assume that the company is more valuable now that it has spun off Warner Media and reduced debt and that it deserves a 12x multiple, it would still be overvalued today.

Keep in mind also that T has not increased its dividend since the spinoff, and organic growth prospects are extremely limited going forward.

The US population is barely growing at all, and while T can push customers toward higher priced premium plans, it must also invest heavily in capex in order to maintain a competitive network. Even fiber, perhaps the fastest growth area for the company, is highly competitive, with many peers also investing heavily to capture market share.

While T's streamlined operations are an improvement from previous years, it remains a fundamentally low-growth company and should be considered as such.

3. Ford (F)

There has been lots of discussion lately about what extent this American automobile stalwart will benefit or be hurt by recent legislative and tariff policy changes.

But beyond the recent headlines, we simply think this stock is not a good long-term hold for dividend investors. The primary reason is because F is a highly cyclical stock.

When the economy is strong, jobs are plentiful, wages are growing, and asset values are rising, people buy cars. When the economy is weak, unemployment is high, wage growth is low, and asset values are in a downturn, people don't buy cars.

That may be a slight oversimplification, but only a slight one.

F has seen lots of ups and downs over the last two decades, but note that its stock price is at basically the same level as it was in 2005.

Moreover, the company's dividend has also been mostly flat over the last two decades:

Note that during recessions, the dividend is suspended entirely.

From 2007 through 2011, F paid no dividends. And from Q2 2020 through Q4 2021, F also paid no dividends.

Given this history, ask yourself what would cause the next 10-20 years to look any different.

Sure, F may slightly benefit from tariff protections and tax deductibility of auto loans, and removing the EV tax credit may also give a slight boost to its mostly ICE and hybrid vehicle offerings. But a recession anytime in the next several years could more than offset all of those benefits.

And, as we mentioned in the section about AT&T, the US population is barely growing, which removes a secular tailwind to car sales.

We think fans of Ford, especially dividend investors, should stick with the cars and not the stock.

3 Stocks To Buy Instead

1. NNN REIT (NNN)

NNN is another net lease REIT like O, but its portfolio is about 13% the size of O's. This gives NNN a significant amount of further room to grow its portfolio without running into the same size-related problems we discussed about O above.

Like O, NNN is a fellow Dividend Aristocrat with a 35-year dividend growth record.

And both REITs currently yield almost exactly the same 5.6%. But in several ways, NNN looks more attractive than O right now.

For one, NNN is valued at around 12.5x AFFO (adjusted funds from operations), whereas O is a turn higher at 13.5x AFFO.

At the same time, NNN's payout ratio is around 70%, whereas O's is a bit higher at 75%. That gives NNN a bit more retained cash to reinvest back into its business or deleveraging.

And while NNN and O have similar levels of leverage (mid-5x net debt-to-EBITDA ratios), NNN's balance sheet is more favorably structured. NNN boasts a weighted average debt maturity of 11.6 years, whereas O's weighted average debt maturity is 6.6 years.

This is a big reason why NNN has outperformed O over the last several years of rising or elevated interest rates.

In an environment where interest rates are no longer falling or ultra-low, NNN is the better investment.

That's why we prefer it to O.

2. Brookfield Infrastructure (BIP, BIPC)

BIP/C is an investment vehicle sponsored and partially owned by parent company Brookfield Asset Management (BAM). The company owns a diversified and global portfolio of infrastructure assets that provide stable, long-term, and inflation-linked cash flow streams.

BIP owns and operates ports, railroads, gas pipelines, toll roads, regulated utilities, intermodal containers, wireless towers, and data centers. These are economically essential assets, many of which benefit from global growth trends such as rising electricity demand, LNG exports, mobile data usage, and data center storage.

These assets typically feature long-term contracts with significant inflation protections. And the company's rapidly growing portfolio of data assets, especially data centers, is a critical piece of its future growth prospects. BIP is actively expanding its number and capacity of data centers to take advantage of the AI and cloud computing megatrend. The company plans to bring about 200 megawatts of data storage capacity online over the next few years.

BIP boasts a 16-year dividend growth record, and its 5.3%-yielding dividend looks poised to continue growing at a 5-7% annual pace going forward.

For dividend growth investors, this combination of a moderately high yield, mid-single-digit (or better) growth, and potential multiple expansion is the sweet spot for a great long-term investment.

3. Permian Resources (PR)

PR is a pure-play oil & gas producer operating in the Permian Basin, especially the Delaware Basin within the Permian. While smaller energy companies like PR do tend to be riskier than their supermajor peers, we think PR has several things going for it that mitigate risk.

Since the COVID-19 crash, PR's management team, especially the co-CEOs Will Hickey and James Walter who joined in 2022, have proven themselves to be extraordinary capital allocators.

They have reduced net debt to EBITDA down to about 1x, which fundamentally reduces risk for a company tied to the price of oil and gas. They have also opportunistically repurchased shares in the $10 range while remaining disciplined and careful with cash. On top of that, management have significantly hedged their balance sheet with oil hedging instruments that further reduces their cash flow volatility.

Aside from deleveraging and opportunistic buybacks, the new management team are steadily building a track record of strong acquisitions as well. For example, in 2023, PR acquired fellow Permian Basin E&P Earthstone in a stock-for-stock deal that turned PR into the second largest pure-play Permian producer. And more recently, PR used some of its accrued cash to acquire ~40,000 net acres of royalties from Occidental Petroleum (OXY).

With each acquisition like this, PR further increases its efficiency of operations and reduces its cost of production.

Also, the management team own over 6% of total shares, making them significantly aligned with shareholders.

The stock currently yields 4.4%, and management plan to allocate 50% of the company's substantial free cash flow to regular dividends going forward. The rest of FCF will go to opportunistic buybacks, acquisitions, deleveraging or special dividends.

We expect to see dividend growth begin over the next year or so.

Bottom Line

We love dividend investing. In fact, we make it our profession at High Yield Investor. But not all dividend stocks are equally attractive or advisable as long-term investments.

While there are some very compelling names out there, others are debatable, and others we wouldn't touch with a ten-foot pole.

Highly cyclical or heavily indebted companies, those with persistently low valuations (and high costs of capital), and/or those with a record of poor capital allocation strike us as being obvious stocks to avoid.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kaspi's Business Continues To Grow Despite Inflation Woes
data.description:
It was just in June that I last talked about Joint Stock Company Kaspi.kz (KSPI). I've made no secret I'm quite fond of the company, both because of their ability to generate income in Kazakhstan and because of their potential as they start to make moves toward Turkey.

The stock has been up somewhat since then, and now we have Q2 earnings released this week, which came in with pretty solid results once again. Between those new earnings, the reaffirmed outlook for the full year, and the details offered in the conference call, I thought it was worth taking another look at Kaspi.

Revenue Growth Continued In Q2

Despite the continued inflationary struggles in Kazakhstan post-COVID, and the high interest rates that go with it as the nation's central bank tries to get it under control, Kaspi's business within the country continued to fire on all cylinders, once again outpacing the previous year handily.

Q2 2024

Q2 2025

Revenue

$1.11 billion

$1.79 billion

Interest Expenses

($272 million)

($404 million)

Pre-Tax Net Margin

49.4%

33.3%

Net Income

$449 million

$479 million

(source: Ex. 99.1 from SEC release)

A lot of the growth is coming in lower margin segments, which is why the net margin is worsening. There are some bright spots to note though on the long-term front within Kazakhstan.

The high-interest rates mean that Kaspi is seeing deposits soar, up fully 207% year over year. While they can make money off depositors, the real hope is that when inflation normalizes and the interest rates come back down, those deposits will be moved into spending, and in Kazakhstan, spending generally means going through Kaspi one way or another, and at a higher margin.

E-commerce overall remained rather strong, but the largest growth was in the e-Grocery business. That's a relatively new business, and they're still scaling up to be available nationwide. But in just five major cities so far, Kaspi is reporting 57% growth year over year, and is now servicing some 1.1 million customers. That's good news, though e-Grocery is necessarily a lower-margin business. Kaspi says they plan to open two more warehouses for that business in the rest of this year.

Overall, Fintech business in Kazakhstan seems to be going pretty strong in spite of the inflation, and with the deposits, Kaspi has potential for some substantial growth when the problem subsides.

Expectation Is Continued Strong Results in Kazakhstan

The rest of 2025 for Kaspi is expected to be strong as well. Estimates are EPS of $3.07 and $3.56, respectively, for the last two quarters. That would give us a full year EPS of $12.49, and a forward P/E ratio of around 6.81. That's strong even for the financial sector, as the median is 10.52.

The forecast is for the Kazakh economy to accelerate through 2025, though inflation continues to remain high. The general strength in the economy should allow Kaspi to keep meeting its targets, and the company reaffirmed their outlook for the rest of the year. The expectation would be consolidated net income growth in the 15% range for the full year.

Kaspi is embedded in almost a ridiculous amount of overall commerce in Kazakhstan. There's really nothing like it, but the company has ambitions to grow that even more, with officers saying during the conference call that they believe Kazakhstan, on the whole, is "underpenetrated."

Turkey: The Risk But Also the Reward

Kaspi's business expansion into Turkey remains a work in progress. Hepsiburada (HEPS) came in with revenue growth, but continued losses, at least on a GAAP basis. It's no surprise that Kaspi's acquisition of the majority of Hepsiburada in January didn't immediately turn profitability around, but it remains a bit of a drag. That's not to say there's not growth potential here.

In the conference call, Kaspi said they're still working to get Hepsiburada's systems up to their standard. That doesn't happen in a few months. Hepsiburada is a big business in its own right, and Kaspi envisions expansion in Turkey going a lot further than just that.

Last time we talked about the plan to buy Rabobank's Turkish subsidiary. That subsidiary doesn't really do anything right now, but it comes with banking licensing rights in Turkey, which is worth quite a bit to Kaspi for their various fintech interests. Kaspi says they still expect that deal to close at some point in the second half of the year.

Kaspi officers are saying they believe Turkey is an "underappreciated opportunity." I'm perfectly willing to appreciate it, personally, but I think we also have to recognize that it comes with risks.

Though I think the Hepsiburada purchase made a lot of sense in the context of trying to get Kaspi into the rather large Turkish economy, the reality is that their situation is wildly different from Kazakhstan. It's a bigger economy, more people, and a heck of a lot more competition. Kaspi almost certainly will be able to use its experience to improve Hepsiburada's bottom line, and they will insinuate themselves into Turkey's economy with the Rabobank deal, but it remains to be seen just how successful they will be in doing that, or how much they might spend chasing after dreams of major Turkish market share.

Kaspi is big, they have a lot of potential, but Turkey is a tough nut to crack. I'm not faulting them for trying, but it's important to continue watching how their Turkish effort unfolds, and how much it ultimately costs, because whether or not we get a proper return from that investment is very much an open question, and likely will be for a while.

Still Comparatively Cheap, And Still High Yielding

In the end, I'm going to reiterate my strong buy rating on Kaspi, because I like their ability to generate cash flow in Kazakhstan, and I believe that will only grow as time goes on. I may be a bit more circumspect about Turkey, because that looks to be a long-term proposition, but the Kazakhstan business should more than make up for it.

Investors should keep a close eye on inflation in Kazakhstan, because once that's under control I expect margins to start improving again, though again certain segments like e-Grocery are just naturally going to be lower margin businesses.

With regard to Turkey, I'd recommend patience, as it may well be years before we start to see meaningful returns. The potential is there, but it's nowhere near a sure thing, and if Kaspi spends too much money chasing after it, that may be a drag on the company at least in the near term.

data.title: Above 6% Yield From GGN And Its Financial Instruments
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield

With markets looking overvalued for common stocks, we are shifting our focus to quality, publicly traded preferred stocks. We are increasing our exposure to fixed income instruments because we believe that this is where the unrealized potential for returns lies. Looking for a good balance between risk and return, we turned to closed-end preferred stocks. They have a high credit rating and offer stable income. We are particularly interested in long-term instruments with high ratings and profit potential. Today, we are looking at GAMCO Global Gold, Natural Resources & Income Trust (NYSE:GGN) and its preferred stock (NYSE:GGN.PR.B). If you are familiar with our articles, you may have read our article from February, but if not, you can review it and follow how the fund is developing.

Fund Overview

Founded in 2005, GGN has $782 million of total net assets and 156 million common shares at the end of the second quarter of 2025. The number of holdings is 229. Its non-leverage expense ratio is 1.3%. In the picture below are shown details of the GGN's portfolio:

56% of the fund's portfolio is in the "Metals and Mining" sector, and 32% in the "Energy and Energy Services" sector. Exxon Mobil Corp, Northern Star Resources Ltd, and Newmont Corp. are among the top ten holdings of GGN.

This CEF's dividend yield is 7.95% and the annualized distribution rate on NAV is 7.84%. GGN is paying its shareholders a $0.36 yearly dividend in the form of $0.0300 monthly distributions.

At the current moment, the market price of GGN is $4.53, with a NAV of $4.59 and a negative one-year Z-score of -0.39. It's currently trading at a 1.31% discount. It has a low leverage of 10%, and the only source of it is the outstanding preferred shares.

Here is a 5-year total return comparison between GGN (the orange line) and its peers:

Preferred Stock

GGN has one preferred stock:

GGN.PR.B is with a current price of $20.65 and a current yield of 6.05%. Its current stripped yield is 6.10%. The call date is passed, so it is callable anytime. GGN issued 4 million shares of GGN.PR.B on 05/02/2013. The stock pays cumulative distributions of 5% per annum, or $1.25 per quarter, in the form of $0.3125 per quarter. GGN.PR.B has one of the safest credit ratings - Moody's gives it an "A2" credit rating.

Conclusion

GGN offers stable income, credit quality, and can be considered a safe investment in the long term. With a credit rating of A2 and a current yield of 6.05%, GGN.PR.B is a suitable instrument for investors seeking a balance between security and stable income.

data.title: Ace River Capital Q2 2025 Quarterly Letter
data.description:
Dear investors,

For the second quarter of 2025, Ace River Capital Partners, L.P. (the "Fund") returned -5.33%. Over the same period, the S&P 500 (SPX) and Russel 2000 (RTY) Indexes returned +4.1% and +3.6%, respectively.

The fund underperformed this quarter, largely due to weakness across several core holdings despite no material changes to long-term business value. We remain focused on what matters: owning a handful of high-quality small-cap businesses with durable economics, competent management, and the ability to reinvest at attractive rates.

Strategy

My goal is to maintain a concentrated portfolio of small and micro-cap companies with unique advantages and growth potential within their respective industries. The aim is to hold them for the long term. I will be focused on companies that have tangible assets with a preference for unique real estate such as special purpose licensed locations (RICK), waterfront property and marinas (HZO), equity participation interest in mineral deposits and mining operations (VOXR). This strategy will usually avoid companies that are involved in software, finance or insurance. Finding such companies requires significant time and effort. Due to the scarcity of suitable opportunities, constraints on time and resources, and opportunity costs, I will take substantial positions in the companies that meet my criteria and pass my risk/reward assessment. I do not subscribe to wide diversification and only research and follow a limited number of companies deeply. I will not spend much time on a company if I am unwilling to take a substantial position and have the conviction to add to that position on weakness. This approach requires a lot of research and patience. There will be long periods of inactivity with few trades, and I will rarely hold more than five companies at a time. This strategy accepts short-term volatility in pursuit of higher, long-term, tax-efficient compounding returns.

Portfolio Updates

RCI Hospitality: RICK is the only publicly traded owner of adult nightclubs in the US. Currently they own 57 clubs across 13 states and an additional 12 sports-bar restaurants with the "Bombshells" concept. With few municipalities issuing new adult entertainment licenses these businesses function as local monopolies with excellent unit economics. These clubs have limited potential buyers with RICK establishing themselves as the buyer of choice for any club owners looking to sell. This provides a long runway for growth.

RICK continues to do what it does best-generate cash, reinvest it intelligently, and grow shareholder value. They remain the dominant consolidator in the gentlemen's club industry, a space with over 500 targets that meet their acquisition criteria and very little real competition. The most recent quarter showed steady operating results and strong free cash flow. The stock remains undervalued relative to normalized earnings power, especially given the capital allocation discipline this management team has shown.

Vox Royalty Corp: Vox continues to build value through disciplined royalty acquisitions and cash-flowing assets in Tier 1 jurisdictions. This quarter brought updates on multiple key projects advancing toward production. With more than 60 royalties, several near-term catalysts, and a capital-light model, Vox is well-positioned to grow free cash flow without the dilution or capex risk that plagues traditional miners. We continue to believe the market underappreciates the earnings power and embedded optionality in the portfolio.

MarineMax: HZO faced pressure this quarter as retail boat sales continue to normalize post-COVID, compounded by higher interest rates. That said, the company is quietly transforming its business model by increasing its mix of service, storage, and marina operations-higher-margin, recurring revenue streams that improve business quality and reduce cyclicality. The market seems focused on the next quarter. We're focused on the next few years. We added slightly to our position at what we believe are attractive long-term prices.

Looking Ahead

The market continues to reward a narrow group of large-cap tech stocks, while small-caps remain broadly ignored. That's fine with us. We think this disconnect is creating some of the best opportunities we've seen in years. Many of the names on our watchlist trade at deep discounts to intrinsic value. We're prepared to act when the setup is right.

We'll continue to stay patient, focused, and selective.

Fund Positions - In Order of Portfolio Weight

RICK, VOXR, HZO, DSHK

Thank you for reading. Please reach out for any reason.

Nicholas D'Agnillo

Nick@AceRiverCapital.com | 401-499-3935

IMPORTANT DISCLOSURES

In General: This letter is for informational purposes only and is intended to provide an overview of the investment strategy and performance of Ace River Capital Partners, L.P. The contents of this letter should not be construed as investment, legal, tax, or other professional advice.

No Offer to Purchase or Sell Securities: This letter does not constitute an offer to sell or a solicitation of an offer to purchase any security or investment product. Any such offer or solicitation may only be made by means of delivery of a confidential private offering memorandum.

No Tax, Legal, Accounting or Investment Advice: The contents of this letter are not intended to be, and should not be construed as, tax, legal, accounting or investment advice. Prospective investors should consult with their own advisors as to the tax, legal, accounting or investment consequences of an investment in any product described in this letter.

Performance Information: The performance information provided in this letter is based on internal calculations and is subject to revision. Performance results may vary depending on the timing of investments and disinvestments, as well as other market conditions.

Past Performance: Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. Different types of investments involve varying degrees of risk, and there can be no assurance that any specific investment will either be suitable or profitable for an investor's portfolio.

Risk of Loss: All investments involve risk and there can be no assurance that any investment strategy will be successful. The value of an investment may fluctuate, and an investor may not get back the amount invested. Changes in economic, political or other market conditions, as well as issuer-specific events, may have a material impact on the value of a security or a portfolio.

Portfolio Guidelines/Construction: The portfolio construction and investment guidelines for Ace River Capital Partners, L.P. are based on a proprietary investment process that seeks to identify undervalued securities and maximize returns while minimizing risk. The portfolio is actively managed and may be changed at any time without notice to reflect changes in market conditions or the manager's assessment of individual security or market prospects.

Index Performance: The performance information provided in this letter may be compared to a benchmark index for purposes of illustration. The benchmark index is not intended to represent the performance of any investment product and does not reflect the expenses associated with the management of an actual portfolio.

Logos, Trade Names, Trademarks and Copyrights: All logos, trade names, trademarks, and copyrights referred to in this letter are the property of their respective owners.

Confidentiality/Distribution of the Letter: The information contained in this letter has been obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but its accuracy is not guaranteed. This letter and its contents are confidential and intended solely for the recipient. It may not be reproduced or redistributed without the prior consent of Ace River Capital Partners, L.P."
